comment_text.sn12.22:2.1: “Stripped of patchwork” is ironic given that this is, in fact, a patchwork discourse. This passage appears at MN 22:42.1, where it has a meaningful context. The following section on arousing energy is another patchwork, this time of passages from elsewhere in the canon on arousing energy.
comment_text.sn12.22:2.2: This sentence is not entirely coherent. The opening phrase is normally followed by “those who”, while alameva (“quite enough”) follows the accusative, not the locative.
comment_text.sn12.22:3.1: Elsewhere we find, “in a well explained teaching and training a lazy person lives in suffering” at AN 1.325:1.1, and “mixed up with bad, unskillful qualities” at AN 3.40:2.11.

root_text.sn12.22:1.2: “Dasabalasamannāgato, bhikkhave, tathāgato catūhi ca vesārajjehi samannāgato āsabhaṁ ṭhānaṁ paṭijānāti, parisāsu sīhanādaṁ nadati, brahmacakkaṁ pavatteti:
root_text.sn12.22:1.3: ‘iti rūpaṁ iti rūpassa samudayo iti rūpassa atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.22:1.4: iti vedanā iti vedanāya samudayo iti vedanāya atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.22:1.5: iti saññā iti saññāya samudayo iti saññāya atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.22:1.6: iti saṅkhārā iti saṅkhārānaṁ samudayo iti saṅkhārānaṁ atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.22:1.7: iti viññāṇaṁ iti viññāṇassa samudayo iti viññāṇassa atthaṅgamo.
root_text.sn12.22:1.8: Iti imasmiṁ sati idaṁ hoti, imassuppādā idaṁ uppajjati;
root_text.sn12.22:1.9: imasmiṁ asati idaṁ na hoti imassa nirodhā idaṁ nirujjhati.
root_text.sn12.22:2.1: Evaṁ svākkhāto, bhikkhave, mayā dhammo uttāno vivaṭo pakāsito chinnapilotiko.
root_text.sn12.22:2.2: Evaṁ svākkhāte kho, bhikkhave, mayā dhamme uttāne vivaṭe pakāsite chinnapilotike alameva saddhāpabbajitena kulaputtena vīriyaṁ ārabhituṁ:
root_text.sn12.22:2.3: ‘kāmaṁ taco ca nhāru ca aṭṭhi ca avasissatu, sarīre upassussatu maṁsalohitaṁ. Yaṁ taṁ purisathāmena purisavīriyena purisaparakkamena pattabbaṁ, na taṁ apāpuṇitvā vīriyassa saṇṭhānaṁ bhavissatī’ti.
root_text.sn12.22:3.1: Dukkhaṁ, bhikkhave, kusīto viharati vokiṇṇo pāpakehi akusalehi dhammehi, mahantañca sadatthaṁ parihāpeti.
root_text.sn12.22:3.2: Āraddhavīriyo ca kho, bhikkhave, sukhaṁ viharati pavivitto pāpakehi akusalehi dhammehi, mahantañca sadatthaṁ paripūreti.
root_text.sn12.22:3.3: Na, bhikkhave, hīnena aggassa patti hoti.
root_text.sn12.22:3.4: Aggena ca kho, bhikkhave, aggassa patti hoti.
root_text.sn12.22:3.6: Tasmātiha, bhikkhave, vīriyaṁ ārabhatha appattassa pattiyā, anadhigatassa adhigamāya, asacchikatassa sacchikiriyāya.
root_text.sn12.22:3.7: ‘Evaṁ no ayaṁ amhākaṁ pabbajjā avañjhā bhavissati saphalā saudrayā.
root_text.sn12.22:3.8: Yesañca mayaṁ paribhuñjāma cīvarapiṇḍapātasenāsanagilānappaccayabhesajjaparikkhāraṁ tesaṁ te kārā amhesu mahapphalā bhavissanti mahānisaṁsā’ti—
root_text.sn12.22:3.10: Attatthaṁ vā hi, bhikkhave, sampassamānena alameva appamādena sampādetuṁ;
root_text.sn12.22:3.11: paratthaṁ vā hi, bhikkhave, sampassamānena alameva appamādena sampādetuṁ;
root_text.sn12.22:3.12: ubhayatthaṁ vā hi, bhikkhave, sampassamānena alameva appamādena sampādetun”ti.

translation_text.sn12.22:1.2: “Mendicants, a Realized One has ten powers and four kinds of self-assurance. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.3: Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.4: Such is feeling, such is the origin of feeling, such is the ending of feeling.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.5: Such is perception, such is the origin of perception, such is the ending of perception.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.6: Such are choices, such is the origin of choices, such is the ending of choices.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.7: Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the ending of consciousness.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.8: When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.9: When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is:
translation_text.sn12.22:1.10: Ignorance is a requirement for choices.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.11: Choices are a requirement for consciousness. …
translation_text.sn12.22:1.12: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.13: When ignorance fades away and ceases with nothing left over, choices cease.
translation_text.sn12.22:1.15: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.
translation_text.sn12.22:2.1: The teaching well-explained thus by me is clarified, revealed, illuminated, and stripped of patchwork.
translation_text.sn12.22:2.2: In this teaching thus well-explained, it is quite enough for a gentleman who has gone forth out of faith to rouse up energy.
translation_text.sn12.22:2.3: ‘Gladly, let only skin, sinews, and tendons remain! Let the flesh and blood waste away in my body! I will not stop trying until I have achieved what is possible by human strength, energy, and vigor.’
translation_text.sn12.22:3.1: A lazy person lives in suffering, mixed up with bad, unskillful qualities, neglecting their heart’s great goal.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.2: But an energetic person lives happily, secluded from bad, unskillful qualities, fulfilling their heart’s great goal.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.3: The best isn’t reached by the worst.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.4: The best is reached by the best.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.5: This spiritual life is the cream, mendicants, and the Teacher is before you.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.6: So you should rouse up energy for attaining the unattained, achieving the unachieved, and realizing the unrealized, thinking:
translation_text.sn12.22:3.7: ‘In this way our going forth will not be wasted, but will be fruitful and fertile.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.8: And the services of those whose robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick we enjoy shall be very fruitful and beneficial for them.’
translation_text.sn12.22:3.10: Considering what is good for yourself, mendicants, is quite enough for you to persist with diligence.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.11: Considering what is good for others is quite enough for you to persist with diligence.
translation_text.sn12.22:3.12: Considering what is good for both is quite enough for you to persist with diligence.”

variant_text.sn12.22:2.3: nhāru → nahāru (bj, sya-all, km); nahārū (pts1ed, pts2ed)
variant_text.sn12.22:3.8: Yesañca → yesaṁ (bj, sya-all, km); yesaṁ hi (pts1ed, pts2ed, mr)
